|  | |

| Screenplay by | [Fabio Pittorru](/wiki/Fabio_Pittorru "Fabio Pittorru")  [Massimo Felisatti](/wiki/Massimo_Felisatti "Massimo Felisatti") |
| Starring | [Antonio Sabàto Sr.](/wiki/Antonio_Sab%C3%A0to_Sr. "Antonio Sabàto Sr.") [Aldo Giuffrè](/wiki/Aldo_Giuffr%C3%A8 "Aldo Giuffrè") [Vittorio Caprioli](/wiki/Vittorio_Caprioli "Vittorio Caprioli") [Elio Pandolfi](/wiki/Elio_Pandolfi "Elio Pandolfi") |

***When Men Carried Clubs and Women Played Ding-Dong*** ([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Quando gli uomini armarono la clava e... con le donne fecero din don*) is a 1971 film directed by [Bruno Corbucci](/wiki/Bruno_Corbucci "Bruno Corbucci").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=When_Men_Carried_Clubs_and_Women_Played_Ding-Dong&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

While two prehistoric tribes are fighting against each other, Listra, a woman, is trying to persuade her 'husband' not to join the war. In order to achieve the same goal with their respective partners, the women of the tribe start a [sex strike](/wiki/Sex_strike "Sex strike").

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=When_Men_Carried_Clubs_and_Women_Played_Ding-Dong&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Production")]

The story is a free adaptation in [Prehistoric times](/wiki/Prehistory "Prehistory") of two comedies by [Aristophanes](/wiki/Aristophanes "Aristophanes"), *[Lysistrata](/wiki/Lysistrata "Lysistrata")* and *[Women at the Thesmophoria](/wiki/Thesmophoriazusae "Thesmophoriazusae")* [[2]](#cite_note-2)
The film was shot between Tor San Lorenzo and Tor Caldera,[[3]](#cite_note-3) in the [Lazio region](/wiki/Lazio "Lazio"), a location usually chosen by Corbucci and other directors for [Spaghetti westerns](/wiki/Spaghetti_Western "Spaghetti Western").[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Critical response

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=When_Men_Carried_Clubs_and_Women_Played_Ding-Dong&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Critical response")]

A contemporary review in the "Segnalazioni Cinematografiche”[[6]](#cite_note-6) saw in it



— a comedy with extremely poor ideas, and that relies upon the lowest kind of comic expedients.

A retrospective review of the English dubbed DVD version distributed by Cheezy Flicks states: "It's not particularly well dubbed, not particularly sexy, and not even nearly as funny as it seems to want to be. However, if you're into low-budget B-movies or you have some weird caveman fetish, then this could very well be the film for you"[[7]](#cite_note-7) while a review of the 2003 DVD released by Something Weird Video finds that "As moviemaking, this is very basic stuff. Most of the film is shot on exterior sets of fairly well-dressed tribal camps. The lighting is basic and the 'scope screen allows directors Corbucci and Campanile to play the action loose. There aren't any special effects, and the actual content of the film is basically 30 hairy guys and sexy gals running around on some barren real estate. *Ding Dong* wants to be funny rather than outright sexy. There's enough nudity to go around, but most of the soft sex scenes cut away from the action, just as older films did. There's a gay 'interior decorator' typed caveman on hand for some cheap digs. If one is deeply into stupid comedy, there might be a laugh here. (...) The picture is just a big curiosity."[[8]](#cite_note-8)
